title: practical english usage - Part 50
content:
becoming common in Britain as well.

British English is changing under American influence, so some of these uses are or I didn't call Bobby yet   I haven't called ... (BrE I haven't called. ..) or Did you eat already?   Have you eaten .. . ? (BrE Have you eaten already?) past-time adverbs like already, yet, ever and before.

In American English, it is also possible to use the simple past with indefinite Lucy just called / has just called. (BrE Lucy has just called.) Uh,  honey, I lost / I've lost the keys (BrE... I've lost the keys.) (BrE Have you heard? Switzerland has declared war...) Did you hear? Switzerland declared / has declared war on Mongolia!

In American English the simple past is often used to give news.

7 American      English annual 'Ten Tor' trek across Dartmoor.

Driving wind and rain forced 600 out of2,500  teenagers to abandon the A woman was Jailed for six months after taking a baby boy from his mother.

An unnamed businessman was shot dead by terrorists...

started     in  the   past    and   are   still   going     on present      perfect     progressive:       actions     and   situations       which That kid has been watching TV non-stop since breakfast.

'Hi! What have you been doing with yourself?' 'I've been trying to write time up to the present.

We often use the present perfect progressive to talk about people's use of their Food prices have been going up steadily all this year.

It's been raining all day. I'm tired of it.

I've been working very hard recently.

which  started in the past and are still going on.

We   use the present perfect progressive to look back over actions and situations 2 continuing        actions      and   situations For double letters in words like sitting, stopping, ►    347.

I haven't been studying very well recently.

I have been thinking about you.  Have you been waiting long?

have/has been + -ing 1  forms 50 present      perfect      progressive        _________ I've given /1 gave your old radio to Philip.

Has Mark phoned?   Did Mark phone?

We (have) heard that you have rooms to let.

present perfect and past are possible with little difference of meaning.

present relevance of a past event, or about the past details? In some cases both very clear-cut. For 900 years the castle has stood on the hill above the village.

How long have you known her?

- How long have you been working with her?
